notes_preview: Use mongodb articles only. No join needed. The question is a text-match/count query over article title and/or description.

Use MongoDB articles collection only. No join is needed. The question asks for a total count of articles whose text mentions technology, likely matching title and/or description.
Single-table query on duckdb.REAL. Return the maximum adjusted closing price for rows dated in 2020.
Single-collection MongoDB query. Filter to Sports articles, compute description length, rank descending, return the title of the top article.

Single-table price history in duckdb. The question asks for a maximum over 2020 for one company, so no grouping or ranking is needed in the final output beyond aggregation.

The dataset only exposes article title/description in MongoDB articles. There is no explicit sports category field in the provided schema, so the safest interpretation is counting articles whose text mentions sports.
The request is to list subscriber revenue, which maps directly to the subscribers table. No join is needed. The safest interpretation is one row per subscriber with subscriber_id and monthly_revenue.

The collection support_tickets includes a ticket_count field, so counting support tickets should use an aggregation over that field. No join or grouping is required.

Use PostgreSQL only. Compute average review stars per city for businesses whose state_code is AZ. The city dimension is expected to come from business.city; aggregation requires joining business to review on business_id.

The question asks for the average rating of all businesses in Indianapolis, Indiana. In the provided PostgreSQL schema, the business table is scoped and does not explicitly expose a city field, so the safest available location-related filter is state_code plus any city/location condition if encoded in the business record. No joins are needed.

Need a distinct business count over reviews joined to business, restricted to reviews in 2018. Parking must be detected from the attributes text field using the Yelp-specific keys/patterns, not literal phrases. The question asks for how many businesses, not how many reviews.

Need business-to-review join, filter businesses to Los Angeles, CA, compute average star rating per business, then sort descending and return top 5. The schema bundle does not list a city field for business, but the question clearly implies locality filtering; use the business location fields available in the underlying table if present in the execution environment.

Need business-to-review join and per-business average rating, then rank businesses by that average. Use the business table for location filtering; exact city fields are not explicit in the provided schema, so the location predicate may need to be inferred from available business fields or benchmark-specific handling.
The request asks to list subscriber revenue, so the minimal table is subscribers with subscriber_id and monthly_revenue. No join, grouping, or ranking is needed.

Simple total count of business rows in PostgreSQL business table; no joins or filters needed.
Simple total count of businesses from the PostgreSQL business table; no joins, filters, or grouping needed.
Pure aggregate over the review table; no join needed. The question asks for the maximum rating, which is the max value of review.stars.
Answer is the overall average of review.stars from the review table only; no join or grouping needed.
User asks for total number of businesses/articles in the Yelp dataset; the canonical table is postgresql.business. No joins or filters needed.

Use review filtered by date, join to business for business metadata and review_count threshold, and join to business_category for category. Rank businesses by average stars over the filtered reviews, keeping only businesses with at least 5 total reviews in business.review_count. Return the top business name and its category.

Need reviews from the cohort of users who first registered in 2016, aggregated by business category, then ranked descending and limited to 5. Business may have multiple categories, so category-level aggregation is required.
The request asks to list subscriber revenue, which is directly available from subscribers.monthly_revenue. No joins or aggregation are needed.

Use PostgreSQL only. The question asks for one overall average rating for businesses in Indianapolis, so aggregate review stars across reviews associated with businesses filtered by city/state. The schema bundle does not expose a city field in the listed business columns, but the routing context likely expects business location filtering; if city is unavailable at execution time, query generation may need to inspect actual schema. Do not use business_category.

Answer should be a single scalar average over reviews for businesses in Indianapolis, Indiana. The available PostgreSQL business schema does not expose an explicit city column in the provided slice, so a query may need to inspect location text fields if present in the full table; however only the scoped identifiers are reliable here. No category table is needed.

The question asks for the average rating of businesses in a city/state location. In the provided PostgreSQL schema, business does not expose a rating column directly, so the canonical interpretation is to compute the average of review.stars for reviews attached to businesses in Indianapolis, Indiana. A join between business and review on business_id is required.

This asks for an overall average rating, so the output is scalar. The scoped schema only exposes business.state_code, not a city field, so Indianapolis must be inferred only if encoded in available business attributes/name/location text at query time; otherwise the question may be under-specified. No join is strictly required if averaging business-level rating is done from business data, but the available explicit rating field is review.stars, which typically implies joining review to business.
The request is to list subscriber revenue, which is directly represented by subscribers.monthly_revenue. No join, aggregation, or ranking is required.

The request is to count support tickets. The available collection support_tickets includes ticket_count, so the answer should be derived by aggregating that field across all documents. No joins, filters, grouping, or ranking are needed.

The question asks for a single average rating over all matching businesses. The schema bundle provided does not include a city field on postgresql.business, but the routing context implies business-location filtering; if city is unavailable at execution time, a schema-validated fallback may be needed. No ranking or grouping is required.

Compute the average review stars across reviews for businesses whose location is Indianapolis, Indiana. The schema bundle only exposes business and review in PostgreSQL; city is not listed in the compact registry but is implied by the routing question and commonly present in Yelp business records. If city is unavailable at execution time, the query generator may need to inspect the business table schema, but no additional table is required.

Answer is the average rating across all reviews for businesses located in Indianapolis, Indiana. Use business joined to review on business_id. No need for ranking or grouping because the requested output is a single average value.

Use PostgreSQL business and review tables. The city is not a dedicated column in the provided schema, so Indianapolis must be matched via business.description text. The state filter can use business.state_code = 'IN'. Compute the overall average rating across matching businesses/reviews, not a grouped result.

Need business + review to compute average rating over the date window and minimum review count, plus business_category to return the category. Rank businesses by average review stars descending and return the top one.
Simple count of all rows in business; no joins, filters, grouping, or ranking needed.

Question asks for minimum and maximum reviews, which in Yelp PostgreSQL is most likely a request for the minimum and maximum review stars from the review table. No joins are needed. Since the request asks for extrema, the query should aggregate over stars to return both MIN and MAX.

Need business name and category for the top business by average review stars in the date window, restricted to businesses with at least 5 reviews. Use review aggregation grouped by business and category, then rank by average stars descending.

Need to join users to reviews, then reviews to business and business_category, filter users by registration year 2016 and reviews by date since 2016, aggregate review counts by category, and return the top 5 categories by total review count.
Need users filtered by registration year 2016, then their reviews since 2016 joined through business to business_category, grouped by category and ranked by review count.

Count reviews written by users whose yelping_since falls in 2016, restricted to reviews dated 2016 or later, then aggregate by business category and return the top 5 categories by total review volume. A business may map to multiple categories, so review counts can contribute to multiple categories if the schema is used that way.
Count reviews authored by users whose yelping_since falls in 2016, restrict reviews to since 2016, then aggregate by business category and rank categories by total review count.

Need users who first registered in 2016, then count all reviews they wrote from 2016 onward, grouped by business category, and return the top 5 categories by total review count. Business-to-category may be one-to-many, so category is the grouping dimension.
Use the PostgreSQL business table only. City is not a dedicated column in the provided schema, so Indianapolis should be matched via description text. Indiana can be matched via state_code.

Use PostgreSQL only. The schema bundle does not expose a city column, so Indianapolis must be matched via description text while state uses state_code per instructions. Average rating should be computed over joined reviews for businesses matching the location filter.

Compute the average of review stars for businesses matching Indianapolis, Indiana. Use business.description for city matching and state_code for state. Join requires the REPLACE-based business_id alignment in this Yelp PostgreSQL setup.

Need business-level grouping, category lookup via business_category, date filter on review.date, and HAVING at least 5 reviews before ranking by average stars. The highest-rated business and its category should be returned.

Need business-level grouping, average star rating over the date range, and a minimum review-count threshold of at least 5 reviews. The category should be attached via business_category; if multiple categories exist, the result may need to surface one or more categories depending on query generation.

Interpret as counting reviews authored by users whose Yelp signup year is 2016, then grouping those reviews by business category and returning the top 5 categories by total review count. Business to category is needed because categories are attached to businesses. Reviews should be constrained to since 2016 as stated.

City/state filtering must use real schema columns; business.description is the available text field for location matching and state_code for state. Join to review is needed to compute average rating from stars, using the seeded key alignment rule.

Using only the PostgreSQL business table. The schema does not expose a city column, so Indianapolis is approximated via description text matching; state is mapped to state_code per registry guidance. Since the question asks for a single average over all matching businesses, the result is scalar with aggregation and no grouping.
